module_mood_contract: practitioner fluency — 'I know how to test, I know how to learn, I know how to encode.' Not confidence-as-performance; competence-as-posture. Mood bar 8+/10 per teaching beat. Watch-for: compliance-feel or credibility-performance.
trace_note: Phase 1 cached (SHA 941625b0 unchanged). Phase 2 regenerated 2026-05-31: file SHA changed from d1e20ffe to 91b63f60. Phase 2 SHA changed from 3f2bce91 to 8841b9f4. Delta: three new lead-in sentences before study/shapes/primitives markers (lines 41-45) and study prompt now caps output at 'top six or so'. Fast-operator callout placement re-verified: callout still at line 47, study prompt now at line 51 (was 49) — correct ordering preserved. All three persona Phase 2 traces regenerated. Mood floors unchanged: all 8+ across all personas and beats.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood contract named and honoured — practitioner fluency lands per beat.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Maintainer block line 117 names mood target explicitly. All three persona sim Phase 2 scores remain >= 8 after regeneration. Mid-layer Phase 2: 9/10 (encoding close lands at invocation). Senior Phase 2: 8/10 (leeway framing prevents compliance-feel). Fast operator Phase 2: 8/10 (callout converts three-prompt sequence into one-go sweep). New lead-in sentences (lines 41-45) add practitioner framing; 'top six or so' cap prevents sprawl. Mood floor unchanged.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood doesn't resolve early — practitioner fluency is M6's contracted end-state; does not pre-empt M7 (awe + ownership).
rules_evaluated.evidence: File closes at personal skill ship + team-kit-via-human-conversation. No awe register, no closing-arc benediction. M7 optional module owns 'agents meet agents' / 'where is this all going?' — this file doesn't touch it. 'What happened' summary (line 103) is artifact-state, not arc-closure. New lead-in sentences don't add any premature resolution register.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Epistemic mood, not punitive — unease = not-knowing, not having-done-wrong.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 gap diagnosis frames the M4/M5 runs as evidence for the next move, not as failures. 'Diagnosis surfaced rules that turned out wrong' (line 29) — empirical, not corrective. No shame register. New Phase 2 lead-in sentences are curiosity-framed ('the kinds of work you repeat run across everything you do').

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Lead with discipline, not failure mode.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise title 'Spot gaps, build the loop' is discipline-lead. Phase headers 'Diff and name the gaps' / 'Author the session-shaper' name positive disciplines. New lead-in sentences lead with discipline ('look wider than the two runs', 'how wide you look and what you choose to package, is yours'). No failure-word titles.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategy-fidelity — M6 arc matches strategy doc.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Strategy doc M6: 'Return to the packaged run. Diff against the un-packaged baseline. Subtler gaps now. Author the second skill — the learning loop packaged. Ship to team kit.' Exercise body delivers exactly this arc. Evals vocabulary held for closing lecture per strategy doc instruction (line 151 watch-for confirms awareness). New lead-ins don't front-run any downstream module's teaching moment.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategic themes recur in exercise body.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Theme 2 (compounding): 'The authoring move repeats... same move, faster' (line 11); 'Cleaning is the compound move that keeps the loop fast' (line 29). Theme 4 (mirror / self-awareness): new lead-in 'Read your own history first. Your Claude Code sessions from every project are sitting on disk' (line 45) — encoding loop as mirror of own practice. Theme 1 (90% correct / the 10%): implicit in gap-diagnosis and verifier-blind-spots framing throughout Phase 2. Theme encoding/capture: Phase 2 arc is the encoding arc from strategy doc. All major themes present; new lead-ins strengthen the mirror/self-awareness theme.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This is an exercise file, not a lecture. Lecture placement is governed by the module shell (spot-gaps-build-the-loop module file). N/A at exercise scope.

Exercise file; lecture dosage rule applies to module shell. M6's mood is practitioner fluency, not contrast-mood — rule 2 targets contrast-mood modules (M5-style) per compendium text. N/A at this scope.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Body teaches the work; trainer manages the room outside text. No authored conversation cues.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No 'compare with the person next to you,' 'tell the room,' 'share with your neighbor,' or equivalent authored conversation cues present anywhere including the new lead-in sentences (lines 41-45). Hard-grep phrase list clean. Body prose is prompt-instruction and artifact-description throughout. New sentences direct student to act on their own stack ('Read your own history first') not to the room.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: New lead-in sentences (lines 41-45) frame the move; they don't encode the forcing function inline. The forcing function — scan sessions, group by work kind, return top six — is in the referenced prompt block (spot-gaps-build-the-loop-study). Body prose describes what comes back ('What comes back is the work you do over and over, grouped and ranked'); the prompt enacts the constraint including the 'top six or so' cap. Same pattern holds for shapes and primitives prompts.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Teaching moment lands — new study→shapes→primitives arc reliably triggers across persona-skill variation.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Mid persona: study→shapes→primitives runs sequentially; 'top six or so' cap means bounded output, ranked head is readable, teaching moment lands at convergence ('One of the work-shapes you drew holds the dominant gap from your two runs', line 59). Senior persona: collapses all three; teaching moment still fires because convergence is in output, not pacing; 'top six or so' cap respected. Fast operator: fast-operator callout at line 47 enables the one-go collapse; bounded output makes the scan-the-head step faster (~5-6 min); teaching moment fires at same convergence. All three personas reach the 'that shape is mine to package' insight. Teaching moment is not skippable: the connection 'drawn shape → skill shape' (line 59) is the bridge between study output and author prompt; skipping it means picking a skill shape without evidence.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity — exercise delivers M6 Big Idea: 'encoding is the third phase of test → learn → encode.'
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 11: 'You tested at M4, learned at M5, and you encode at M6.' Direct match to strategy doc M6 Big Idea statement. The authoring arc (Phase 1 diff + Phase 2 encoding) enacts the three-phase arc rather than narrating it. New lead-ins reinforce encoding frame ('Read your own history first... few engineers ever read them back' — the encoding move is about surfacing hidden pattern).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Module-to-module arc — exercise close hands off correctly to lecture handoff and team-kit accretion theme.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Lines 99–103: skill ships personally at ~/.claude/skills/session-shaper/SKILL.md; team-kit-via-human-conversation framing at line 101; 'What happened' summary closes the exercise and naturally hands to the closing lecture (the-loop-has-a-name) which gives evals their full weight. No premature arc closure. M7 optional path implicitly preserved by 'agents don't unilaterally change shared team infrastructure' (line 101). New lead-in sentences don't touch the close; handoff intact.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Fast operator callout lifts the known stress case to >=8 mood floor; new lead-in sentences don't dampen it.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Fast operator persona sim (Phase 2 regenerated): three new lead-in sentences (lines 41-45) sit between Phase 2 header and the callout; fast operator skims body prose, so these sentences are invisible to them. Eyes land on callout (line 47) before study prompt (line 51). Callout text is explicit ('Paste them one after another in the same conversation, study then shapes then primitives, and let the answers land together'). The 'top six or so' cap in study prompt means bounded output — scan-the-head step slightly faster for fast operator. Mood floor 8/10 unchanged. Callout placement (line 47 < line 51) verified in current file. Dampening risk: none. New sentences are invisible to fast operator and the cap is a marginal improvement.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Leeway framing ('how wide you look and what you package is yours') encodes M6 agency without collapsing into compliance-feel.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 43: 'How wide you look, and what you choose to package, is yours.' (new sentence, part of the three new lead-ins). Line 51: 'How far you take this is yours.' Both instances explicitly hand latitude to the student before the prompt. The leeway framing is now earlier (line 43) and reinforced (line 51), preventing compliance-feel at both the macro-framing level and the study-output level. Senior persona particularly responsive (sim note: 'how wide you look is yours — yes, exactly').

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Three-skill-shape menu (sharpened verifier / LLM-judge / gap-finder) lands as recognition, not prescription.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Lines 67–74: menu introduced after primitives prompt (line 63), not before. Ordering matters: primitives → menu means student arrives at the shape list with codebase context in hand. Line 73: 'Pick the one the diff points at.' Student-as-diagnostician, not typologist. Unchanged from prior evaluation; new lead-ins don't affect ordering.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Minor finding: two pacing callouts use different register and address slightly overlapping scope.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 47: 'Fast operator?' covers study+shapes+primitives (three prompts). Line 65: 'Solo or fluent in this move?' covers author+critique+invoke (three prompts). Both are opt-up, not mandated. Fast operator persona notices the register shift but is not blocked. This is a TODO-level observation, not a blocking finding. The two callouts address genuinely different audiences (pace vs. familiarity) and cover non-overlapping prompt groups. Unchanged from prior evaluation.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Consider aligning callout register: either both role-addressed ('Fast operator? / Expert?') or both capability-addressed ('Fluent in the wider scan? / Fluent in this move?'). Consistency removes the micro-confusion for fast readers scanning for merge cues.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: New lead-in: 'few engineers ever read them back' — minor over-generalisation, senior-facing friction.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 45: 'Your Claude Code sessions from every project are sitting on disk, and few engineers ever read them back.' Senior persona reads this as slightly presuming they don't read their history (they do). Not blocking: (a) the statement is accurate for most engineers at this experience level in the room; (b) the senior dismisses it and moves on; (c) the sentence functions as a conversion trigger for the mid-layer persona (directly prompts the study scan action); (d) the body prose is not telling the senior they haven't read — it's framing the wider opportunity. Mid-level session-mood benefit outweighs senior-friction cost. TODO-level observation.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Optional polish: 'Most engineers never read their sessions back' softens the over-generalisation without losing the conversion function. Not required.
